policy_statement: Investigate ending oil heating by 2035 and improving renewable energy use and energy efficiency in electrically heated buildings, including selecting a pilot area and examining options with residents.
mechanism_description: The City will investigate ending oil heating and increasing renewable energy use and energy efficiency in electrically heated buildings, including by selecting a pilot area to examine options with residents, to reduce building-related emissions.
typology_evidence_quote: investigate ending oil heating by 2035 and increasing the use of renewable energy and the energy efficiency in electrically heated buildings
primary_causal_pathway: Building heating fuel choice/technology shift → ↓ fossil fuel combustion in buildings (and/or ↓ carbon intensity of heat) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Buildings switch heating fuels/technologies away from oil (and other fossil heating) toward electrified heating, district energy, and/or renewable heat, often paired with efficiency upgrades to reduce set heat.  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced direct combustion of fossil fuels and/or use of lower-carbon heat supply; efficiency reduces total heat demand, further lowering energy use and emissions.  
(3) Outcome: Lower building-sector CO₂e emissions (scope 1 reductions, plus potential scope 2 reductions depending on electricity/heat mix).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from replacing fossil-fuel-based building heating with lower-carbon energy sources.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: switching heating fuels/technologies away from oil reduces fossil combustion and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while efficiency and renewable-energy uptake can also be framed as “resource efficiency,” the dominant intended outcome across this mechanism is emissions reduction from decarbonizing heat (a direct mitigation lever).  
3) Edge cases applied: if electrification increases electricity demand on a carbon-intensive grid, near-term emissions benefits can be smaller; however the mechanism’s primary intent/effect remains mitigation via decarbonized heat supply and reduced direct combustion.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any policy whose core mechanism is building heating fuel switching is primarily aimed at reducing GHG emissions from building operations.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., electrification vs. renewable thermal vs. district energy; inclusion of efficiency measures) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impact depends on the marginal electricity/district-heat mix; policies that mainly add efficiency without meaningful fuel switching could fit Resource Efficiency, but those would be a different canonical mechanism than fuel switching. Fuel switching to fossil gas (from oil) can reduce CO₂ modestly but may increase methane leakage; still primarily mitigation.
key_indicators: “investigate ending oil heating by 2035”; “increasing the use of renewable energy”; “energy efficiency in electrically heated buildings”; “A pilot area will be selected”; “options will be examined together with the residents”
co_benefits: Improved indoor comfort/health, lower local air pollution, reduced energy costs/energy poverty, energy security (not explicit enough to classify separately)
instance_edge_case_notes: “by 2035” resembles a target-setting/phase-out commitment, but the operative verb is “investigate” and the main deliverable is a pilot and option assessment, so it fits better as a planning-strategy rather than a regulatory mandate or finalized target.
